content.full_text:
This letter Keys Advice Letter No. A-14-013 supersedes in totality the Hentschke Advice Letter, 
No. I-08-174. 
 
 
 
March 20, 2014 
 
 
Lacey E. Keys 
Olson Hagel & Fishburn LLP 
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425 
Sacramento, CA  95814 
 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
 
Our File No.  I-14-013 
 
Dear Ms. Keys: 
 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).1  This letter is based on the facts presented.  The Fair Political 
Practices Commission (“the Commission”) does not act as a finder of fact when it renders 
assistance.  (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)  Because your questions are general in 
nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2  Also, please note that our 
advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act.   
 
Your law firm represents several clients that are both 501(c)(3) organizations and 
lobbyist employers.  On their behalf, you are posing multiple questions regarding recently 
adopted amendments to the gift provisions.  We address each of your questions in turn.    
 
 
 
 
 
 
                                                          
 
1  The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014.  All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.  The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations.  All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
 
 
2  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice.  (Section 83114, Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 
 


File No. I-14-013 
Page No. 2 
 
 
 
ANALYSIS 
 
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials,3 the Act regulates the 
receipt of gifts by public officials in three ways: 
 
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials.  The 
current limit is $440 from a single source in a calendar year.  (Section 89503; Regulation 
18940.2.)  This gift limit applies to all elected state and local officials or other individuals 
designated in Section 87200; all candidates for state, local, or judicial office; and any employee 
designated in his or her agency’s conflict-of-interest code, as adopted pursuant to Section 87300, 
if the employee would be required to disclose the receipt of income or gifts from the source of 
the gift on his or her statement of economic interest.  (Section 89503.) 
 
 
Secondly, the Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that 
any gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source received during the calendar year 
are disclosed on the officials’ statements of economic interest.  Reporting requirements apply to 
all officials listed in Section 87200 (Section 87202), all candidates for an office specified in 
Section 87200 (Section 87201), and employees designated in an agency’s conflict-of-interest 
code as specified in the code (Section 87302(b)). 
 
 
Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the 
donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $440 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made.  (Sections 
87100, 87103(e), Regulations 18700, 18703.4.) 
 
1.  Is a gift that meets one of the exceptions to gift rules under both Section 89506(a) and 
Regulation 18950.1 reportable? 
 
 
Notwithstanding certain exceptions, a gift is “any payment that confers a personal benefit 
on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received.”  
(Section 82028.)  While payments meeting the criteria of Section 89506(a) are not prohibited or 
limited, the payments are gifts and must be reported.  (See Regulation 18942(b)(1) and 
18950(a).)  However, in adopting Regulation 18950.1, the Commission identified certain 
payments that do not confer a personal benefit on the official and do not constitute gifts or 
income to an official because of the nature in which the payments are made.  Because there is no 
personal benefit, any payment meeting the criteria of Regulation 18950.1 and reported pursuant 
to the terms thereof is not a gift, would not be analyzed under Section 89506(a), and is not 
reportable. 
 
In interpreting revised Regulation 18950.1 for the first time, we also take the opportunity 
to address the reporting obligations for lobbyist employers making a payment meeting the 
                                                          
3  A “public official” is “every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local governmental 
agency.”  (Section 82048.)   


File No. I-14-013 
Page No. 3 
 
 
 
criteria of the regulation.  Previously, we have advised that a payment is not considered a 
reportable activity expense for a lobbyist employer under Sections 86115 and 86116 so long as a 
payment fell within an exception to the gift rules.  (See Micheli Advice Letter, No. I-93-424, “ 
intrastate travel provided directly in connection with a speech given by a public official is not 
deemed a gift and is not reportable by any filer, including a lobbyist employer;” and Blessing 
Advice Letter, No. A-01-068, lobbyist employer did not have to report payments for officials’ 
airport parking and shuttle services as activity expenses if the recipient agency followed gift to 
agency provisions (former Regulation 18944.2).)  Moreover, we do not find that the Commission 
intended to supersede past advice regarding lobbyist reporting rules in adopting the revised gift 
regulations.  Accordingly, payments meeting the criteria of Regulation 18950.1 are not 
reportable activity expenses for a lobbyist employer.  Nonetheless, reporting is not lost because 
these payments must be reported by the agency accepting the payments under the very terms of 
the regulation (Regulation 18950.1(a)(6) and (f)) and must be included by a lobbyist employer in 
reporting the “total of all other payments to influence legislative or administrative action” (see 
Section 86116(i); Regulation 18616(f)).   
 
2.  Does a “payment of travel” under Regulation 18950(b)(1) include food provided at a 
meeting or conference if the official walks or drives to the event? 
 
 
As provided in Regulation 18950, a “payment for travel” for purposes of Section 89506 
and Regulations 18950.1 through 18950.3 is “any payment that provides transportation to an 
official from one location to another location as well as a payment for lodging and food 
connected with the travel.”  As clarified in Staff’s Memorandum to the Commission, Proposed 
Amendments to Regulation 18944 and Travel Regulations 18950 through 18950.4, dated August 
12, 2013, the proposed definition of travel was not intended to “have a mileage limit.”  While the 
memorandum specifically stated that both a “taxi ride down the street” as well as an “airplane 
trip around the world” qualified as travel, these examples are not limiting, and we see no 
distinction between a “taxi ride down the street” and walking or driving to the event.  
Accordingly, the definition of “payment of travel” for purposes of these provisions includes food 
provided at a meeting or conference even if the official walks or drives to the event.   
 
3.  How does a lobbyist employer verify that the Section 18950.1 exception applies prior 
to the deadline for the agency to report the payment or if the agency is under the $2,500 filing 
threshold?  Are there any additional precautions the lobbyist employer must take if the payment 
is made for travel by an elected officer or an official designated in Section 87200? 
 
Regulation 18950.1 provides criteria for determining when payments for travel made in 
conjunction with official agency business have no personal benefit to an official and do not 
constitute gifts or income to the official.  The regulation requires the agency to maintain a report 
detailing any payment and to make that report available for public inspection even if the 
payments received by the agency are under the $2,500 threshold for filing with the Commission.  
A lobbyist employer must treat a payment as a gift to the official unless the agency has advised 
the lobbyist employer that it is reporting the payment pursuant to the regulation. 
  


File No. I-14-013 
Page No. 4 
 
 
 
4.  Are “stakeholder” meetings hosted by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, which 
include refreshments and lunches, held at the request of a state agency regarding a pending state 
regulatory issue that includes state agency officials (possibly from more than one agency), 
representatives of private interests, and possibly local and federal agency officials “working 
groups” for purposes of Regulation 18950.1(c)(6)?   
 
The “working group” provisions provide a limited exception for food and refreshments 
when the host is providing a forum for the collaborative efforts of multiple interests, which 
includes governmental officials.  The “working group” exception does not permit an 
organization to provide food and beverages to a governmental official merely for meeting with 
the official.   
 
You have asked whether “stakeholder” meetings, requested by a state agency, regarding a 
pending state regulatory issue constitute “working groups,” but you have not specifically 
identified the interests or entities invited or attending the meetings.  Generally, so long as the 
“stakeholder” meetings are open and attended by a wide variety of independent interests and 
entities, the meetings are “working groups” within the exception.  Assuming that all other 
elements of Regulation 18950.1 have been met, the payments for refreshments and lunches are 
not considered gifts when provided by the host of the meeting and the payments are made 
equally to all attendees at the meeting.   
 
However, to the extent that a meeting is not open and attended by a variety of 
independent interest or entities, it is not possible to provide a determination of whether the 
meeting constitutes a “working group” without additional information.  In these circumstances, it 
is advisable that you seek additional assistance providing a description of the meeting and 
indentifying the attendees of the meeting.     
 
5.  May a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization provide on-site transportation; meals and 
refreshments; a bag containing a hat, sunscreen, and snacks; and lodging the night before early 
departure as the sponsor of a tour of project areas, which have received state funding, for the 
purpose of demonstrating how the funds were used and for providing the basis for future 
requests for additional funding under Regulation 18950.1(c)(7)?   
 
Regulation 18950.1(c)(7) provides a limited exception for travel payments “required to 
attend a location to view an in place operation, structure, facility, or available product where the 
viewing would substantially enhance an official's knowledge and understanding in making an 
informed decision to enter into a contract regarding a similar operation, structure, facility or 
purchase the product pursuant to the jurisdictional authority of the official's governmental 
employer.”  So long as the travel payments provide the basis for future requests for additional 
funding as you have stated, the payments fall within the exception in Regulation 18950.1(c)(7) 
assuming all other elements of the regulation are meet.   
 
Note, however, travel payments are limited to transportation and lodging and food 
connected with the travel.  (Regulation 18950(b)(1).)  Accordingly, any other items such as a hat 


File No. I-14-013 
Page No. 5 
 
 
 
or sunscreen do not fall under the narrow exception and must be considered gifts to the official.  
Moreover, we caution that the duration of travel under the exception is limited to that necessary 
to accomplish the purposes for which the travel was provided as determined by the governmental 
employer using the same standards imposed for travel paid with government funds.  (Regulation 
18950.)  As you have asked us to assume that all other elements of Regulation 18950.1 have 
been met, for the purpose of this analysis, we have assumed that the night stay is consistent with 
the agency’s policy for travel paid with governmental funds.         
 
6.  For a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization hosting a conference on climate change, which 
will be attended by representatives from environmental organizations across the country, elected 
state officials, state agency officials, federal officials and members of the public with an interest 
in climate change: 
 
 Are payments for state and agency officials (other than the two state officials 
presenting) to attend the conference including necessary travel and food exempt from 
gift limits under Section 89506(a)? 
 
In pertinent part, Section 89506(a) states: 
 
 
“Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual 
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to a 
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or 
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of 
the following apply:  
 
[¶]…[¶] 
 
“(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a 
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private 
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation 
Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside the 
United States which substantially satisfies the requirements for tax-exempt status 
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.  
 
A conference on climate change is reasonably related to a legislative or 
governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or international public policy.  
Accordingly, payments by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization for an official’s actual 
transportation to the conference and related lodging and subsistence are not prohibited or 
limited.  However, while exempt from gift limits, the gifts are reportable.  (See 
Regulation 18942(b)(1) and 18950(a).)4   
                                                          
4 It is important to note that payments do not qualify for the exceptions for travel payments merely because 
the payments are made during the course of the trip.  Under Section 89506, payments must be reasonably related to 
a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or international public policy to qualify for 


File No. I-14-013 
Page No. 6 
 
 
 
 
 Are payments to attend the conference including necessary travel and food for the 
Secretary of the California Environmental Protection Agency and the Chairman of 
the California Air Resources Control Board exempt from gift provisions under 
Regulations 18950.1(c)(4) if the official’s present a seminar and (g) considering that 
the conference will be widely attended and held at a hotel with rates that are higher 
than the allowable state per diem rate? 
  
Regulation 18950.1(c)(4) provides a limited exception for “travel expenses of an official 
to an educational conference directly related to the governmental employer’s functions or duties 
under the laws that it administers,” if the official is a named presenter at the conference, and the 
payment is made by the organizers of the event.  For the Secretary of the California 
Environmental Protection Agency and the Chairman of the California Air Resources Control 
Board, a climate change conference is directly related to their employers’ functions or duties 
under the laws that they administer.  Assuming that all other elements of Section 18950.1 are 
met, payments for necessary travel and food are not considered gifts.   
 
In regard to rates higher than the state per diem rate, “travel expenses are generally 
limited to no more than the expenses allowable for travel for agency business that would 
reasonably be paid at agency expense” (Regulation 18950.1(e)(2)).  However, travel expenses 
are not limited when the “lodging and food is provided at a site where the official attends a 
widely attended meeting or conference and the value is substantially equivalent in value to the 
lodging or food typically made available to the other attendees” (Regulation 18950.1(g)) and 
food “accepted for attendance at an event where food is provided as part of the admission to the 
event” (Regulation 18950.1(b)).   
 
Under the facts you have provided, the conference will be widely attended and held at a 
hotel with rates exceeding the state per diem rate.  Accordingly, assuming all other elements of 
Regulation 18950.1 are met, Regulation 18950.1(g) permits payments for lodging at the higher 
rate because these are the rooms available to other attendees.   
 
 Are payments for nominal items with the organization’s logo (e.g. pens, mugs, etc.) 
gifts to the public official attendees, but not gifts to the state officials presenting 
under Regulation 18942(a)(11)?    
 
The definition of “gift” does not include a rebate or discount that “is made in the regular 
course of business to members of the public without regard to official status.”  (Section 82028.)  
                                                                                                                                                                                           
the exception from the gift limit.  We have previously advised that payments by a 501(c)(3) organization for an 
official to attend a private concert and to take a tour of a historical battleship, while attending a conference in 
France, were gifts to the official subject to both the gift limit and reporting, despite the fact that payments by the 
organization for the official’s airfare, hotel accommodations, and meals were only reportable and not subject to the 
gift limit under Section 89506. (Federighi Advice Letter, No. A-07-139.)  In addition, we note that reportable gifts 
not subject to gift limits may still result in disqualification from governmental decisions affecting the source under 
Sections 87100 and 87103(e).   


File No. I-14-013 
Page No. 7 
 
 
 
Based upon the facts provided, the climate conference will be widely attended and open to both 
governmental officials and private interests.  Under these facts, nominal items provided to 
officials that are also provided to all other attendees are made in the regular course of business 
without regard to official status.  Accordingly, so long as the items are provided to all in 
attendance, the items are not gifts to officials in attendance whether speaking or not.   
 
In regard to Regulation 18942(a)(11), it is unnecessary to consider this exception in light 
of our conclusion above.  However, we note that this regulation does establish an additional 
exception to the gift rules for “admission, where paid admission is required, food, and nominal 
items provided as part of the paid admission” provided to an official giving a speech5 at an event 
by the person organizing the event.  A nominal” item is an “insignificant item typically 
purchased in large volume and provided for free as a means of advertisement at events . . ..”6     
 
Notwithstanding 18942(a)(11),  
 
7.  For a lobbyist employer, which is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization operating 
hospital: 
 
 Are appetizers, beverages, and a pen with the hospital’s name and logo provided at a 
grand opening ceremony of a new hospital, open to the entire community, to a state 
assembly member giving a speech at the ceremony exempt from gift provisions under 
Regulation 18942(a)(11) if the items are provided to all attendees? 
 
As more fully discussed in Question 6, food and nominal items provided to an official 
speaking at an event are not gifts to the official so long as the food and nominal items are offered 
to all attendees.  (Regulation 18942(a)(11).)  Based on the facts provided, appetizers, beverages, 
and a pen provided at a hospital grand opening ceremony, open to the entire community, to a 
state assembly member speaking at the event are not gifts to the assembly member.     
 
 Is lunch provided to a state senator at a meeting to discuss the implementation of the 
Affordable Care Act or other legislation that applies to healthcare providers exempt 
from gift provisions under Regulation 18942(a)(11) if the legislator makes a 
presentation on the subject and the same lunch is provide to all attendees?    
 
As more fully discussed in Question 6, food and nominal items provided to an official 
speaking at an event are not gifts to the official so long as the food and nominal items are offered 
to all attendees.  (Regulation 18942(a)(11).)  Based on the facts provided, lunch provided to a 
                                                          
5  A “speech” includes “making a speech, participating on a panel, or making a substantive formal 
presentation at a seminar or similar event.”  (Regulation 18950(b)(2).)  
 
6  Notably, the regulation extends the exception to food and nominal items provided as part of the paid 
attendance.  However, there is no reason to distinguish food and nominal items provided by the organizer to a 
speaker at an event if admission is free.  We read Regulation 18942(a)(11) to permit food and nominal items even 
when paid admission is not required so long as the food and nominal items are offered to all attendees.   


File No. I-14-013 
Page No. 8 
 
 
 
legislator making a presentation on the Affordable Care Act is not a gift so long as the lunch is 
provided to all attendees and the presentation is a “speech” as defined by Regulation 
18950(b)(2).  We caution that the presentation constitutes a speech only if made at a seminar or 
other similar event.  Generally, so long as representatives of many independent interests or 
entities are attending the meeting the presentation would constitute a speech.  However, a 
presentation at a meeting with limited attendance or merely between the host and official would 
not constitute a speech for purposes of Regulation 18942(a)(11).    
 
 Are benefits provided to an official including parking validation, a boxed lunch, and 
bottled water exempt from gift provisions under the “working group” exception in 
Regulation 18950.1(c)(6) if the organization invites state and local elected officials to 
tour its facilities related to a specific issue such as information regarding a medical 
specialty and the various services offered by a particular hospital, demonstrating the 
effectiveness of medical records technology, or to highlight a healthcare issue 
affecting care providers or patients considering?  Are the payments exempt if made to 
the official for a follow up meeting at which representatives of the employer and the 
official discuss the specific issue and various legislative or administrative solutions, 
improvements?   
As more fully discussed in Question 4, the “working group” exception in Regulation 
18950.1(c)(6) does not permit an organization to provide food and beverages to a governmental 
official merely for meeting with the official.  Parking validation, a lunch, and bottled water 
provided at a tour or at a follow up meeting would not fall within the “working group” exception 
and would constitute gifts to the official.   
 
8.  For a lobbyist employer, which is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, are benefits 
provided to an official including parking validation, a boxed lunch, and bottled water exempt 
from gift provisions as “training” under Regulation 18950.1(c)(5) if the organization invites the 
official to visit hospital locations to attend a presentation regarding the delivery of care in the 
community? 
 
Regulation 18950.1(c)(5) provides a limited exception for “travel expenses of an official 
for the purpose of receiving training directly related to the official's job duties and the payment is 
provided by an organization that commonly provides such training.”  So long as the hospital falls 
within the official’s jurisdiction, parking validation, lunch, and a bottle water provided to 
officials attending a presentation regarding the delivery of care in the community falls within the 
exception of Regulation 18950.1(c)(5) and are not gifts assuming that all other elements of 
Section 18950.1 are met. 
 
 
 
 
 
 


File No. I-14-013 
Page No. 9 
 
 
 
 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Sincerely,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
General Counsel 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
/s/ Brian G. Lau 
 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Counsel, Legal Division 
 
BGL:jgl

sections.question: Your law firm represents several clients that are both 501(c)(3) organizations and lobbyist employers. On their behalf, you are posing multiple questions regarding recently adopted amendments to the gift provisions. Questions include: (1) Is a gift that meets one of the exceptions to gift rules under both Section 89506(a) and Regulation 18950.1 reportable? (2) Does a "payment of travel" under Regulation 18950(b)(1) include food provided at a meeting or conference if the official walks or drives to the event? (3) How does a lobbyist employer verify that the Section 18950.1 exception applies prior to the deadline for the agency to report the payment or if the agency is under the $2,500 filing threshold? Are there any additional precautions the lobbyist employer must take if the payment is made for travel by an elected officer or an official designated in Section 87200? (4) Are "stakeholder" meetings hosted by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, which include refreshments and lunches, held at the request of a state agency regarding a pending state regulatory issue that includes state agency officials "working groups" for purposes of Regulation 18950.1(c)(6)? (5) May a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization provide on-site transportation; meals and refreshments; a bag containing a hat, sunscreen, and snacks; and lodging the night before early departure as the sponsor of a tour of project areas under Regulation 18950.1(c)(7)?
sections.analysis:
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials,3 the Act regulates the 
receipt of gifts by public officials in three ways: 
 
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials.  The 
current limit is $440 from a single source in a calendar year.  (Section 89503; Regulation 
18940.2.)  This gift limit applies to all elected state and local officials or other individuals 
designated in Section 87200; all candidates for state, local, or judicial office; and any employee 
designated in his or her agency’s conflict-of-interest code, as adopted pursuant to Section 87300, 
if the employee would be required to disclose the receipt of income or gifts from the source of 
the gift on his or her statement of economic interest.  (Section 89503.) 
 
 
Secondly, the Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that 
any gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source received during the calendar year 
are disclosed on the officials’ statements of economic interest.  Reporting requirements apply to 
all officials listed in Section 87200 (Section 87202), all candidates for an office specified in 
Section 87200 (Section 87201), and employees designated in an agency’s conflict-of-interest 
code as specified in the code (Section 87302(b)). 
 
 
Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the 
donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $440 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made.  (Sections 
87100, 87103(e), Regulations 18700, 18703.4.) 
 
1.  Is a gift that meets one of the exceptions to gift rules under both Section 89506(a) and 
Regulation 18950.1 reportable? 
 
 
Notwithstanding certain exceptions, a gift is “any payment that confers a personal benefit 
on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received.”  
(Section 82028.)  While payments meeting the criteria of Section 89506(a) are not prohibited or 
limited, the payments are gifts and must be reported.  (See Regulation 18942(b)(1) and 
18950(a).)  However, in adopting Regulation 18950.1, the Commission identified certain 
payments that do not confer a personal benefit on the official and do not constitute gifts or 
income to an official because of the nature in which the payments are made.  Because there is no 
personal benefit, any payment meeting the criteria of Regulation 18950.1 and reported pursuant 
to the terms thereof is not a gift, would not be analyzed under Section 89506(a), and is not 
reportable. 
 
In interpreting revised Regulation 18950.1 for the first time, we also take the opportunity 
to address the reporting obligations for lobbyist employers making a payment meeting the 
                                                          
3  A “public official” is “every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local governmental 
agency.”  (Section 82048.)    
 
 
 
criteria of the regulation.  Previously, we have advised that a payment is not considered a 
reportable activity expense for a lobbyist employer under Sections 86115 and 86116 so long as a 
payment fell within an exception to the gift rules.  (See Micheli Advice Letter, No. I-93-424, “ 
intrastate travel provided directly in connection with a speech given by a public official is not 
deemed a gift and is not reportable by any filer, including a lobbyist employer;” and Blessing 
Advice Letter, No. A-01-068, lobbyist employer did not have to report payments for officials’ 
airport parking and shuttle services as activity expenses if the recipient agency followed gift to 
agency provisions (former Regulation 18944.2).)  Moreover, we do not find that the Commission 
intended to supersede past advice regarding lobbyist reporting rules in adopting the revised gift 
regulations.  Accordingly, payments meeting the criteria of Regulation 18950.1 are not 
reportable activity expenses for a lobbyist employer.  Nonetheless, reporting is not lost because 
these payments must be reported by the agency accepting the payments under the very terms of 
the regulation (Regulation 18950.1(a)(6) and (f)) and must be included by a lobbyist employer in 
reporting the “total of all other payments to influence legislative or administrative action” (see 
Section 86116(i); Regulation 18616(f)).   
 
2.  Does a “payment of travel” under Regulation 18950(b)(1) include food provided at a 
meeting or conference if the official walks or drives to the event? 
 
 
As provided in Regulation 18950, a “payment for travel” for purposes of Section 89506 
and Regulations 18950.1 through 18950.3 is “any payment that provides transportation to an 
official from one location to another location as well as a payment for lodging and food 
connected with the travel.”  As clarified in Staff’s Memorandum to the Commission, Proposed 
Amendments to Regulation 18944 and Travel Regulations 18950 through 18950.4, dated August 
12, 2013, the proposed definition of travel was not intended to “have a mileage limit.”  While the 
memorandum specifically stated that both a “taxi ride down the street” as well as an “airplane 
trip around the world” qualified as travel, these examples are not limiting, and we see no 
distinction between a “taxi ride down the street” and walking or driving to the event.  
Accordingly, the definition of “payment of travel” for purposes of these provisions includes food 
provided at a meeting or conference even if the official walks or drives to the event.   
 
3.  How does a lobbyist employer verify that the Section 18950.1 exception applies prior 
to the deadline for the agency to report the payment or if the agency is under the $2,500 filing 
threshold?  Are there any additional precautions the lobbyist employer must take if the payment 
is made for travel by an elected officer or an official designated in Section 87200? 
 
Regulation 18950.1 provides criteria for determining when payments for travel made in 
conjunction with official agency business have no personal benefit to an official and do not 
constitute gifts or income to the official.  The regulation requires the agency to maintain a report 
detailing any payment and to make that report available for public inspection even if the 
payments received by the agency are under the $2,500 threshold for filing with the Commission.  
A lobbyist employer must treat a payment as a gift to the official unless the agency has advised 
the lobbyist employer that it is reporting the payment pursuant to the regulation.  
 
 
 
4.  Are “stakeholder” meetings hosted by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, which 
include refreshments and lunches, held at the request of a state agency regarding a pending state 
regulatory issue that includes state agency officials (possibly from more than one agency), 
representatives of private interests, and possibly local and federal agency officials “working 
groups” for purposes of Regulation 18950.1(c)(6)?   
 
The “working group” provisions provide a limited exception for food and refreshments 
when the host is providing a forum for the collaborative efforts of multiple interests, which 
includes governmental officials.  The “working group” exception does not permit an 
organization to provide food and beverages to a governmental official merely for meeting with 
the official.   
 
You have asked whether “stakeholder” meetings, requested by a state agency, regarding a 
pending state regulatory issue constitute “working groups,” but you have not specifically 
identified the interests or entities invited or attending the meetings.  Generally, so long as the 
“stakeholder” meetings are open and attended by a wide variety of independent interests and 
entities, the meetings are “working groups” within the exception.  Assuming that all other 
elements of Regulation 18950.1 have been met, the payments for refreshments and lunches are 
not considered gifts when provided by the host of the meeting and the payments are made 
equally to all attendees at the meeting.   
 
However, to the extent that a meeting is not open and attended by a variety of 
independent interest or entities, it is not possible to provide a determination of whether the 
meeting constitutes a “working group” without additional information.  In these circumstances, it 
is advisable that you seek additional assistance providing a description of the meeting and 
indentifying the attendees of the meeting.     
 
5.  May a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization provide on-site transportation; meals and 
refreshments; a bag containing a hat, sunscreen, and snacks; and lodging the night before early 
departure as the sponsor of a tour of project areas, which have received state funding, for the 
purpose of demonstrating how the funds were used and for providing the basis for future 
requests for additional funding under Regulation 18950.1(c)(7)?   
 
Regulation 18950.1(c)(7) provides a limited exception for travel payments “required to 
attend a location to view an in place operation, structure, facility, or available product where the 
viewing would substantially enhance an official's knowledge and understanding in making an 
informed decision to enter into a contract regarding a similar operation, structure, facility or 
purchase the product pursuant to the jurisdictional authority of the official's governmental 
employer.”  So long as the travel payments provide the basis for future requests for additional 
funding as you have stated, the payments fall within the exception in Regulation 18950.1(c)(7) 
assuming all other elements of the regulation are meet.   
 
Note, however, travel payments are limited to transportation and lodging and food 
connected with the travel.  (Regulation 18950(b)(1).)  Accordingly, any other items such as a hat  
 
 
 
or sunscreen do not fall under the narrow exception and must be considered gifts to the official.  
Moreover, we caution that the duration of travel under the exception is limited to that necessary 
to accomplish the purposes for which the travel was provided as determined by the governmental 
employer using the same standards imposed for travel paid with government funds.  (Regulation 
18950.)  As you have asked us to assume that all other elements of Regulation 18950.1 have 
been met, for the purpose of this analysis, we have assumed that the night stay is consistent with 
the agency’s policy for travel paid with governmental funds.         
 
6.  For a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization hosting a conference on climate change, which 
will be attended by representatives from environmental organizations across the country, elected 
state officials, state agency officials, federal officials and members of the public with an interest 
in climate change: 
 
 Are payments for state and agency officials (other than the two state officials 
presenting) to attend the conference including necessary travel and food exempt from 
gift limits under Section 89506(a)? 
 
In pertinent part, Section 89506(a) states: 
 
 
“Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual 
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to a 
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or 
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of 
the following apply:  
 
[¶]…[¶] 
 
“(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a 
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private 
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation 
Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside the 
United States which substantially satisfies the requirements for tax-exempt status 
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.  
 
A conference on climate change is reasonably related to a legislative or 
governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or international public policy.  
Accordingly, payments by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization for an official’s actual 
transportation to the conference and related lodging and subsistence are not prohibited or 
limited.  However, while exempt from gift limits, the gifts are reportable.  (See 
Regulation 18942(b)(1) and 18950(a).)4   
                                                          
4 It is important to note that payments do not qualify for the exceptions for travel payments merely because 
the payments are made during the course of the trip.  Under Section 89506, payments must be reasonably related to 
a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or international public policy to qualify for  
 
 
 
 
 Are payments to attend the conference including necessary travel and food for the 
Secretary of the California Environmental Protection Agency and the Chairman of 
the California Air Resources Control Board exempt from gift provisions under 
Regulations 18950.1(c)(4) if the official’s present a seminar and (g) considering that 
the conference will be widely attended and held at a hotel with rates that are higher 
than the allowable state per diem rate? 
  
Regulation 18950.1(c)(4) provides a limited exception for “travel expenses of an official 
to an educational conference directly related to the governmental employer’s functions or duties 
under the laws that it administers,” if the official is a named presenter at the conference, and the 
payment is made by the organizers of the event.  For the Secretary of the California 
Environmental Protection Agency and the Chairman of the California Air Resources Control 
Board, a climate change conference is directly related to their employers’ functions or duties 
under the laws that they administer.  Assuming that all other elements of Section 18950.1 are 
met, payments for necessary travel and food are not considered gifts.   
 
In regard to rates higher than the state per diem rate, “travel expenses are generally 
limited to no more than the expenses allowable for travel for agency business that would 
reasonably be paid at agency expense” (Regulation 18950.1(e)(2)).  However, travel expenses 
are not limited when the “lodging and food is provided at a site where the official attends a 
widely attended meeting or conference and the value is substantially equivalent in value to the 
lodging or food typically made available to the other attendees” (Regulation 18950.1(g)) and 
food “accepted for attendance at an event where food is provided as part of the admission to the 
event” (Regulation 18950.1(b)).   
 
Under the facts you have provided, the conference will be widely attended and held at a 
hotel with rates exceeding the state per diem rate.  Accordingly, assuming all other elements of 
Regulation 18950.1 are met, Regulation 18950.1(g) permits payments for lodging at the higher 
rate because these are the rooms available to other attendees.   
 
 Are payments for nominal items with the organization’s logo (e.g. pens, mugs, etc.) 
gifts to the public official attendees, but not gifts to the state officials presenting 
under Regulation 18942(a)(11)?    
 
The definition of “gift” does not include a rebate or discount that “is made in the regular 
course of business to members of the public without regard to official status.”  (Section 82028.)  
                                                                                                                                                                                           
the exception from the gift limit.  We have previously advised that payments by a 501(c)(3) organization for an 
official to attend a private concert and to take a tour of a historical battleship, while attending a conference in 
France, were gifts to the official subject to both the gift limit and reporting, despite the fact that payments by the 
organization for the official’s airfare, hotel accommodations, and meals were only reportable and not subject to the 
gift limit under Section 89506. (Federighi Advice Letter, No. A-07-139.)  In addition, we note that reportable gifts 
not subject to gift limits may still result in disqualification from governmental decisions affecting the source under 
Sections 87100 and 87103(e).    
 
 
 
Based upon the facts provided, the climate conference will be widely attended and open to both 
governmental officials and private interests.  Under these facts, nominal items provided to 
officials that are also provided to all other attendees are made in the regular course of business 
without regard to official status.  Accordingly, so long as the items are provided to all in 
attendance, the items are not gifts to officials in attendance whether speaking or not.   
 
In regard to Regulation 18942(a)(11), it is unnecessary to consider this exception in light 
of our conclusion above.  However, we note that this regulation does establish an additional 
exception to the gift rules for “admission, where paid admission is required, food, and nominal 
items provided as part of the paid admission” provided to an official giving a speech5 at an event 
by the person organizing the event.  A nominal” item is an “insignificant item typically 
purchased in large volume and provided for free as a means of advertisement at events . . ..”6     
 
Notwithstanding 18942(a)(11),  
 
7.  For a lobbyist employer, which is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization operating 
hospital: 
 
 Are appetizers, beverages, and a pen with the hospital’s name and logo provided at a 
grand opening ceremony of a new hospital, open to the entire community, to a state 
assembly member giving a speech at the ceremony exempt from gift provisions under 
Regulation 18942(a)(11) if the items are provided to all attendees? 
 
As more fully discussed in Question 6, food and nominal items provided to an official 
speaking at an event are not gifts to the official so long as the food and nominal items are offered 
to all attendees.  (Regulation 18942(a)(11).)  Based on the facts provided, appetizers, beverages, 
and a pen provided at a hospital grand opening ceremony, open to the entire community, to a 
state assembly member speaking at the event are not gifts to the assembly member.     
 
 Is lunch provided to a state senator at a meeting to discuss the implementation of the 
Affordable Care Act or other legislation that applies to healthcare providers exempt 
from gift provisions under Regulation 18942(a)(11) if the legislator makes a 
presentation on the subject and the same lunch is provide to all attendees?    
 
As more fully discussed in Question 6, food and nominal items provided to an official 
speaking at an event are not gifts to the official so long as the food and nominal items are offered 
to all attendees.  (Regulation 18942(a)(11).)  Based on the facts provided, lunch provided to a 
                                                          
5  A “speech” includes “making a speech, participating on a panel, or making a substantive formal 
presentation at a seminar or similar event.”  (Regulation 18950(b)(2).)  
 
6  Notably, the regulation extends the exception to food and nominal items provided as part of the paid 
attendance.  However, there is no reason to distinguish food and nominal items provided by the organizer to a 
speaker at an event if admission is free.  We read Regulation 18942(a)(11) to permit food and nominal items even 
when paid admission is not required so long as the food and nominal items are offered to all attendees.    
 
 
 
legislator making a presentation on the Affordable Care Act is not a gift so long as the lunch is 
provided to all attendees and the presentation is a “speech” as defined by Regulation 
18950(b)(2).  We caution that the presentation constitutes a speech only if made at a seminar or 
other similar event.  Generally, so long as representatives of many independent interests or 
entities are attending the meeting the presentation would constitute a speech.  However, a 
presentation at a meeting with limited attendance or merely between the host and official would 
not constitute a speech for purposes of Regulation 18942(a)(11).    
 
 Are benefits provided to an official including parking validation, a boxed lunch, and 
bottled water exempt from gift provisions under the “working group” exception in 
Regulation 18950.1(c)(6) if the organization invites state and local elected officials to 
tour its facilities related to a specific issue such as information regarding a medical 
specialty and the various services offered by a particular hospital, demonstrating the 
effectiveness of medical records technology, or to highlight a healthcare issue 
affecting care providers or patients considering?  Are the payments exempt if made to 
the official for a follow up meeting at which representatives of the employer and the 
official discuss the specific issue and various legislative or administrative solutions, 
improvements?   
As more fully discussed in Question 4, the “working group” exception in Regulation 
18950.1(c)(6) does not permit an organization to provide food and beverages to a governmental 
official merely for meeting with the official.  Parking validation, a lunch, and bottled water 
provided at a tour or at a follow up meeting would not fall within the “working group” exception 
and would constitute gifts to the official.   
 
8.  For a lobbyist employer, which is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, are benefits 
provided to an official including parking validation, a boxed lunch, and bottled water exempt 
from gift provisions as “training” under Regulation 18950.1(c)(5) if the organization invites the 
official to visit hospital locations to attend a presentation regarding the delivery of care in the 
community? 
 
Regulation 18950.1(c)(5) provides a limited exception for “travel expenses of an official 
for the purpose of receiving training directly related to the official's job duties and the payment is 
provided by an organization that commonly provides such training.”  So long as the hospital falls 
within the official’s jurisdiction, parking validation, lunch, and a bottle water provided to 
officials attending a presentation regarding the delivery of care in the community falls within the 
exception of Regulation 18950.1(c)(5) and are not gifts assuming that all other elements of 
Section 18950.1 are met.
sections.question_synthetic: A law firm representing 501(c)(3) organizations and lobbyist employers seeks guidance on recent amendments to gift provisions under the Political Reform Act, including questions about reportability of excepted gifts, definition of travel payments, verification procedures for exceptions, classification of stakeholder meetings as working groups, and permissible sponsor-provided items for project tours.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provides detailed guidance on each question: (1) Payments meeting Regulation 18950.1 criteria are not gifts and not reportable by officials or lobbyist employers; (2) Travel payments include food even if officials walk/drive to events; (3) Lobbyist employers must rely on agency certification of compliance with Regulation 18950.1; (4) Stakeholder meetings constitute "working groups" if open and attended by diverse independent interests; and (5) Sponsors may provide transportation, meals, and lodging but not ancillary items like hats or sunscreen under Regulation 18950.1(c)(7).
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains multiple question-and-answer sections (5 numbered questions). Verbatim conclusion section does not exist; conclusions are integrated into analysis sections for each question. Document is modern format informal advice letter (File No. I-14-013 redesignated as A-14-013). No immunity provided due to informal nature (per Section 83114). Some content truncated in source material.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your law firm represents several clients that are both 501(c)(3) organizations and lobbyist employers. On their behalf, you are posing multiple questions regarding recently adopted amendments to the gift provisions. Questions include: (1) Is a gift that meets one of the exceptions to gift rules under both Section 89506(a) and Regulation 18950.1 reportable? (2) Does a "payment of travel" under Regulation 18950(b)(1) include food provided at a meeting or conference if the official walks or drives to the event? (3) How does a lobbyist employer verify that the Section 18950.1 exception applies prior to the deadline for the agency to report the payment or if the agency is under the $2,500 filing threshold? Are there any additional precautions the lobbyist employer must take if the payment is made for travel by an elected officer or an official designated in Section 87200? (4) Are "stakeholder" meetings hosted by a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, which include refreshments and lunches, held at the request of a state agency regarding a pending state regulatory issue that includes state agency officials "working groups" for purposes of Regulation 18950.1(c)(6)? (5) May a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization provide on-site transportation; meals and refreshments; a bag containing a hat, sunscreen, and snacks; and lodging the night before early departure as the sponsor of a tour of project areas under Regulation 18950.1(c)(7)?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC provides detailed guidance on each question: (1) Payments meeting Regulation 18950.1 criteria are not gifts and not reportable by officials or lobbyist employers; (2) Travel payments include food even if officials walk/drive to events; (3) Lobbyist employers must rely on agency certification of compliance with Regulation 18950.1; (4) Stakeholder meetings constitute "working groups" if open and attended by diverse independent interests; and (5) Sponsors may provide transportation, meals, and lodging but not ancillary items like hats or sunscreen under Regulation 18950.1(c)(7).
embedding.first_500_words: This letter Keys Advice Letter No. A-14-013 supersedes in totality the Hentschke Advice Letter, No. I-08-174. March 20, 2014 Lacey E. Keys Olson Hagel & Fishburn LLP 555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-14-013 Dear Ms. Keys: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (“the Commission”) does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. Your law firm represents several clients that are both 501(c)(3) organizations and lobbyist employers. On their behalf, you are posing multiple questions regarding recently adopted amendments to the gift provisions. We address each of your questions in turn. 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114, Regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. I-14-013 Page No. 2 ANALYSIS In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials,3 the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by public officials in three ways: First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The current limit is $440 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.) This gift limit applies to all elected state and local officials or other individuals designated in Section 87200; all candidates for state, local, or judicial office; and any employee designated in his or her agency’s conflict-of-interest code, as adopted pursuant to Section 87300, if the employee would be required to disclose the receipt of income or gifts from the source of the gift on his or her statement of economic interest. (Section 89503.) Secondly, the Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source received during the calendar year are disclosed on the officials’ statements of economic interest. Reporting requirements apply to all officials listed in Section 87200 (Section 87202), all candidates for an office specified in Section 87200 (Section 87201), and employees designated in an agency’s conflict-of-interest code as specified in the code (Section 87302(b)). Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the
embedding.summary: This informal advice letter addresses questions from a law firm representing 501(c)(3) organizations and lobbyist employers regarding recently amended gift provisions under California's Political Reform Act, providing guidance on reportability, travel payment definitions, exception verification procedures, working group classifications, and permissible sponsor-provided items for project tours. The letter supersedes prior advice letter I-08-174.
